Facts.text: Battle of the Trebia: Hannibal's Carthaginian army heavily defeats Roman forces on Italian soil during the Second Punic War
Ildefonsus, Archbishop of Toledo, and later saint, reportedly has a vision of the Virgin Mary, who presents him with a priestly vestment
Alfonso I of Aragon occupies Zaragoza defeating taking Moorish Taifa Zaragoza from the Moors
Etienne Aubert elected as Pope Innocentius VI
Peace of Tournai (Dutch - Doornik) signed between the Burgundian Duke Philip II and the rebellious city of Ghent
Anton of Burgundy becomes Duke of Brabant
First fleet of the Dutch East India Company under Admiral Steven van der Haghen departs for the East-Indies
English parliament accept unanimously, Protestation
Thomas Fleet publishes "Mother Goose's Melodies For Children" in Boston, Massachusetts
Empress Maria Theresa expels Jews from Prague, Bohemia and Moravia
1st US national Thanksgiving Day, commemorating British General Burgoyne's surrender
Surrender of the frigate La Lutine by French royalists to Lord Hood; renamed HMS Lutine, she later becomes a famous treasure wreck
1st US newspaper to appear on Sunday (Baltimore Monitor)
British take Fort Niagara in War of 1812
William Bond obtains 1st photograph of Moon through a telescope
Battle at Lexington, Tennessee (part of Forrest's Second Raid)
1st US cattle importation law passed
Around 49 victims of "Angola Horror" train wreck burn to death in Angola, New York
Canada's Hamilton Football Club plays its 1st game
French SS Byzantin sinks after collision in Dardanelles, 210 killed
John Kehoe, the last of the Molly Maguires, is executed in Pennsylvania
Richard Wetherill and his brother in-law discover the ancient Anasazi ruins of Mesa Verde, Colorado
Frederick Lugard's British expedition to Meng and Kampala, Uganda
Day 4 of the 1st cricket test Australia v England, England following on 4-268, 7 in front
First automobile speed record set of 63 kph (39 mph) by French Count Gaston de Chasseloup-Laubat of Paris
Field Marshal Lord Roberts appointed British supreme commander in South Africa
The Upper Ferntree Gully to Gembrook Narrow-gauge (2 ft 6 in or 762 mm) Railway (now the Puffing Billy Railway) in Victoria, Australia is opened for traffic.
H V Hordern takes 8-81 in 2nd innings of First Class debut (NSW v Qld)
The Indian Inquiry Commission, also known as the Solomon Commission, commences its sittings in Pretoria
Battle of Verdun, longest of World War I, officially ends in German defeat after nine months of fighting and almost 1 million total casualties
1st US postage stamps printed without the words United States or US
International zone of Tangier set up in Morocco
The Lanka Sama Samaja Party is founded in Ceylon
Queensland cricket all out for 49 v Vic, Fleetwood-Smith 7-17
Su-Lin, 1st giant panda to come to US from China, arrives in San Francisco
Finnish army recaptures Agläjärvi (Karelia)
44 surviving crewmen of German U-434 surrender to the HMS Blankney after scuttling their wounded submarine. Two are lost.
Japanese troops land on Hong Kong
Nazi occupiers of Amsterdam destroy electricity plants
US Destroyers Hull, Spence & Monaghan sink in typhoon (Philippines)
Uruguay joins the United Nations
TV's 1st network dramatic serial "Faraway Hill" ends 2 month run in US
Indonesia begins its 2nd political election
WDSU TV channel 6 in New Orleans, LA (NBC) begins broadcasting
Ellis W. Ryan resigns as Cleveland Indians president
KATV TV channel 7 in Little Rock, AR (ABC) begins broadcasting
KMID TV channel 2 in Midland & Odessa, TX (ABC) begins broadcasting
"To Tell the Truth" debuts on CBS-TV
Israeli flag hoisted on Mount Sinai
Japan admitted to UN
World's first full scale nuclear power plant, for peacetime use only, begins to generate electricity at the Shippingport Atomic Power Station in Pennsylvania
Niger gains autonomy within French Community (National Day)
Project SCORE, the world’s first communications satellite, is launched from Cape Canaveral
General Meeting of UN condemns apartheid
India annexes Portuguese colonies of Goa, Damao & Diu
KAIL TV channel 53 in Fresno, CA (IND) begins broadcasting
Ballon d'Or: Dukler Prague midfielder Josef Masopust wins award for best European football player; beats Benfica striker Eusébio and FC Köln defender Karl-Heinz Schnellinger
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
WAIQ TV channel 26 in Montgomery, AL (PBS) begins broadcasting
Muskegon, Michigan gets 3' of snow
Ron Clarke runs world record 10k (28:15.6)
"The Pink Panther" cartoon series premieres (Pink Phink)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Kenneth LeBel jumps 17 barrels on ice skates
Saturn's moon Epimetheus is discovered by Richard L. Walker.
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
USSR performs nuclear test at Eastern Kazakh, Semipalitinsk USSR
"On Her Majesty's Secret Service", 6th James Bond Film starring George Lazenby and Diana Rigg, premieres in London
House of Lords votes to abolish the death penalty in England, Wales and Scotland (Northern Ireland 25 July 1973)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Polish uprising fails
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
1st Candlelight Processional at the EPCOT Center, Disney World
CBS radio cancels Saturday morning band concerts
Three members of the Irish Republican Army die when the bomb they were transporting explodes prematurely in King Street, Magherafelt, County Derry.
US dollar devalued 7.9% in Holland ($1=Ÿ3,245)
Soyuz 13 launched into Earth orbit for 8 days
Yanks sign Dick Williams as manager, overturned later by AL Pres
San Francisco Visitors Center at City Hall opens
"Wonder Woman" debuts on ABC
Soviet dissident Viktor Bukovskiexchanged for Chile CP-leader Corvalan
NBA Cleveland Cavaliers retire jersey # 42, Nate Thumond
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
USSR performs underground nuclear test

Stuntman Stanley Barrett unofficially breaks the land speed record and the sound barrier in his three-wheeled vehicle the Budweiser Rocket (739.666 mph or Mach 1.01). The speed was never officially recorded and the attempt remains controversial.
Dutch 2nd Chamber accepts minister Van Agts abortion laws
IRA's Sean McKenna becomes critically ill, ends hunger strike
Vietnam adopts constitution
Flight readiness firing of Challenger's main engines; 20 seconds
NBA San Diego Clippers ends 29 game road losing streak
UN Security Council unanimously condemns "acts of hostage-taking"
American stockbroker Ivan Boesky sentenced to 3 years in prison for insider trading
Seattle Seahawks win their 1st ever division title with 9-7 record
"I Love Lucy" Christmas episode, shown for 1st time in over 30 years
Athol Fugard's play "My Children, My Africa" premieres in NYC
Bush reiterates his "no concessions" stance against Iraq
NL announces Buffalo, Denver, Miami, Orlando, Tampa-St Petersburg, & Washington, D.C. as 6 finalist for 1993 expansion (Miami & Denver win)
DeForest Kelly (Dr McCoy on Star Trek) gets a star in Hollywood
General Motors announces closing of 21 plants
FCC vote 4-1 to allow Infinity to purchase Cook Inlet stations
Kevin Scott skates world record 1K (1:12.54)
The MGM Grand Las Vegas hotel and casino first opens as the largest hotel complex in the world (since surpassed)
Socialist Party (ex-communist) wins Bulgaria parliamentary election

During a press conference, Iranian Deputy Foreign Minister Abbas Maleki states that Iran supports the free flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz, but reserves the option of closing off the shipping route if it is threatened
Start of 1st Test Cricket match between Zimbabwe & England
The Oakland, California school board passes a resolution officially declaring "Ebonics" a language or dialect
US TV industry execs agree to adopt a ratings system
HTML 4.0 is published by the World Wide Web Consortium
Environmental activist Julia Butterfly Hill ends her protest after 738 days of living in a redwood tree in Humboldt County
NASA launches the Terra platform into orbit, carrying five Earth observation instruments, including ASTER, CERES, MISR, MODIS, and MOPITT
Ballon d'Or: Liverpool forward Michael Owen is named best football player in Europe ahead of Real Madrid striker Raúl and Bayern Munich goalkeeper Oliver Kahn
Governor of California Gray Davis announces the state faces a record budget deficit of $35 billion, roughly double the figure reported during his reelection campaign one month earlier
The last US troops withdraw from Iraq, formally ending the Iraq War
4 people are killed and 11 are injured after an apartment block collapses in Palermo, Italy
Canadian police begin arresting 17 people for 'the Great Maple Syrup Heist', for stealing 3,000 tonnes from a storage facility in Quebec
The Seleka rebel coalition kills 15 people during its capture of Bria, the Central African Republic
Australia regains cricket's Ashes against England, winning by an innings to take 3 game lead at the WACA in Perth
Fire destroys 600 stilt homes in Manaus, northwestern Brazil
Manchester United sacks high profile Portuguese manager Jose Mourinho; United in 6th place in EPL, 19 points behind league leaders Liverpool; Ole Gunnar Solskjaer new caretaker manager
Meteor explodes in huge fireball over the Bering Sea with 10 times the energy of Hiroshima atomic bomb, 2nd largest in last 30 yrs
Nevada becomes the first US state with a female-majority legislature with new appointments taking it to 51%
International body Covax, established to distribute COVID-19 vaccines to low and middle income countries, announces deals with vaccine manufacturers and method for countries to share excess vaccines
Moderna COVID-19 vaccine granted emergency authorization by US Food and Drug Administration
South Africa announces detection of new variant of COVID-19, called 501.V2, which is driving a second wave of infections
6.2 magnitude earthquake strikes Jishishan county in Gansu Province killing at least 127 people - China's worst earthquake in 13 years
BP joins other companies in pausing transit of ships through the Red Sea, amid attacks by Yemen's Houthi rebels on commercial vessels, with serious implications for global shipping
Extreme rainfall and flooding after Cyclone Jasper breaks 100-year old weather records around Cairns and Daintree in northern Queensland, Australia
Icelandic volcano erupts on Reykjanes peninsula, 40km from country's capital Reykjavik
Utah YouTube vlogger Ruby Franke pleads guilty to child abuse charges, including convincing her children they are evil and possessed
Mongol EmperorKublai Khanrenames his empire "Yuan" (元 yuán), marking the start of the Yuan Dynasty of China
Abel Tasman's expedition is the first European voyage to reach New Zealand sailing into Wharewharangi Bay at the top of the South Island, making the first confirmed contact with Maori
A skirmish at Grass Cove in Queen Charlotte Sound results in the deaths of two Māori and nine members ofJames Cook's expedition, New Zealand
British KingGeorge IIIdismissesDuke of Portland's Whig government after eight months
George Washington's body interred at Mount Vernon
HMS Beagle withCharles Darwinaboard reaches Tierra del Fuego for the first time
John William Drapertakes 1st portrait photograph of a female face made in US
George Hamilton-Gordon(Peelite party) becomes Prime Minister of the United Kingdom after the downfall of the Conservative government ofEdward Smith-Stanley
Anton Bruckner's 8th Symphony premieres at the Vienna Musikverein, with Hans Richter leading the Vienna Philharmonic
Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's ballet "The Nutcracker" premieres in Saint Petersburg, Russia, now the world's most performed ballet; his final opera "Isolanta" also premieres
British parliament passes the Education Act, which will come to be regarded as the most important legislation ofArthur Balfour's government
The Soviet government issues a decree recognizing Finland's newly-declared independence [N.S. December 31]
Edvard Benešbecomes President of Czechoslovakia afterTomáš Masarykretires
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Optissima Pax
Ballon d'Or: Blackpool & England winger Stanley Matthews wins inaugural award for best football player in Europe; beats Real Madrid pairAlfredo Di Stéfano&Raymond Kopa
Phil Rizzutosigns as NY Yankees radio-TV announcer
Sammy Baughnamed 1st coach of NY Titans (AFL)
For second consecutive year, AP namesWilma Rudolphfemale athlete of year
Dmitri Shostakovich's 13th Symphony premieres in Moscow
"The Pink Panther" film premieres, directed byBlake Edwards, and starringPeter SellersandDavid Niven, with theme music composed byHenry Mancini
During funeral service held for soul singerSam Cooke, fans cause damage to funeral home

Filming is completed for "Star Trek" pilot "The Cage"; cast includes Jeffrey Hunter, Susan Oliver,Leonard Nimoy, and Majel Barret; although it never airs, some footage re-used in "Menagerie" episode of the series
Frank BormanandJim Lovellsplash down in the Atlantic after two week Gemini 7 mission

Dr. Seuss' "How the Grinch Stole Christmas!" airs for 1st time on CBS; animated special directed by Chuck Jones, features voices ofBoris Karloff, June Foray, and Thurl Ravenscroft, with songs by Albert Hague
Musical-fantasy film "Chitty Chitty Bang Bang", co-written byRoald Dahland director Ken Hughes, starringDick Van DykeandSally Ann Howes, with songs by Sherman and Sherman, premieres in NYC
Operation PUSH (People United To Save Humanity) formed by Rev.Jesse Jacksonin Chicago, Illinois
Second remake of "A Star is Born" film, directed by Frank Pierson and starringBarbra StreisandandKris Kristofferson, premieres
Darryl Strawberrypleads not guilty on tax evasion charges
Romantic comedy film "You've Got Mail", starringTom HanksandMeg Ryan, directed byNora Ephron, and written by sisters Nora and Delia Ephron, is released
"The Hours" premieres in the US, based on the novel by Michael Cunningham and starringMeryl Streep,Julianne MooreandNicole Kidman
"The Two Towers", 2nd of theLord of the Ringsfilms is released. Makes US$62 opening weekend and $926 million total worldwide.
Fashion designerCalvin Kleinannounces he is selling his company to shirt-maker Phillips-Van Heusen for $430 million
James Cameron's "Avatar" starringSam Worthington, Zoe Saldana andSigourney Weaver, released in the US, becomes highest-grossing film of all time
Miguel Ángel Félix Gallardois transferred from a maximum security prison to a medium security facility in Guadalajara due to declining health
Austria swears in new coalition government withSebastian Kurzaged 31 of the People's Party made Chancellor
South Africa's ANC selectsCyril Ramaphosaas leader, to succeed PresidentJacob Zuma
US House of Representatives votes to impeach PresidentDonald Trumpfor abuse of power (230-197) and obstruction of Congress (229-198)
US Vice PresidentMike Pencereceives the Pfizer/BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine live on TV

Cinderella's glass slipper really a witty literary joke, byCharles Perrault, poking fun at French KingLouis XIV's love of shoes and Versailles' Hall of Mirrors according to Scottish professor Genevieve Warwick

FIFA World Cup Final, Lusail Stadium, Qatar: Argentina beats France, 4-2 in penalty shootout after teams locked at 3-3 after extra time;Lionel Messiscores 2 for Argentina,Kylian Mbappéa hat-trick for France
Pope Franciswill allow priests to bless same-sex couples, though not when connected to a civil or same-sex union
New Jersey becomes the third state to ratify the US constitution
Piltdown Man, later discovered to be a hoax, is supposedly found in the Piltdown Gravel Pit, by Charles Dawson
The 18th Amendment to the US Constitution, authorizing prohibition of alcohol, is approved by the US congress and sent to the states for ratification
US launches Operation Linebacker II, its heaviest bombing of North Vietnam, as negotiations to end the Vietnam War collapse
6 health workers dispensing polio vaccinations are gunned down in Pakistan
Albert VI, Archduke of Austria, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1463)
Ōuchi Yoshitaka, Japanese warlord, born in Yamaguchi, Japan (d. 1551)
Philipp Dulichius, German composer, born in Chemnitz, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1631)
Jakob Hassler, German composer, born in the Free Imperial City of Nuremberg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1622)
Simonds d'Ewes, English antiquarian and politician who wrote an account of the Long Parliament, born in Coaxdon Hall, Dorset (d. 1650)
Charles du Fresne du Cange, French scholar and philologist, born in Amiens, France (d. 1688)
Heinrich Roth, German Sanskrit scholar, born in Dillingen, Germany (d. 1668)
Adriaen Millaert [Melar], Flemish engraver, baptized at Antwerp (d. 1667)
Willem van de Velde the Younger, Dutch seascape painter, baptized in Leiden (d. 1707)
Antonius Matthaeus, Dutch lawyer and historian (De criminibus), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1710)
Christopher Polhem, Swedish scientist who invented early industrial machinery for mining, born in Tingstäde, Sweden (d. 1751)
James Douglas, 2nd Duke of Queensberry, Scottish statesman who as Lord High Commissioner negotiated 1707 Treaty of Union, born in Scotland  (d. 1711)
Wenzel Ludwig von Radolt, Austrian composer and lutenist, born in Vienna. Austria (d. 1716)
Charles Wesley, English Methodist leader (brother of John Wesley) and hymn writer (Hark! The Herald Angels Sing), born in Epworth, England (d. 1788)
Johann Salomo Semler, German historian and Bible commentator, born in Saalfeld, duchy of Saxe-Saalfeld (d. 1791)
Jean-Baptiste Rey, French conductor (Paris Opera) and composer, born in Lauzerte, France (d. 1810)
Knud Lyne Rahbek, Danish literary historian, writer, and critic (Om sku skuespillerkunsten), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1830)
Camille Pleyel, Austrian pianist, piano manufacturer, and music publisher, born in Strasbourg, France (d. 1855)
William Allen, American politician (Rep, Governor of Ohio 1874-76), born in Edenton, North Carolina (d. 1879)
Alexander Sandor Asboth, Hungarian-American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Keszthely, Hungary (d. 1868)
Wiktor Kazynski, Polish composer, born in Vilnius, Russian Empire (d. 1867)
Alfred Elzey Jones, American soldier, Major General in the Confederate Army, born in Somerset County, Maryland (d. 1871)

Charles Griffin, American Major General (Union Army), born in Granville, Ohio (d. 1867)
Alexandre Chatrian [wrote as Erckmann-Chatrian jointly with Émile Erckmann], French writer (L'ami Fritz), born in Abreschviller, France (d. 1890)
Mercer Brooke John, American naval official, pioneered deep sea soundings, commander in the Confederate Navy, born in Fort Brooke, Florida (d. 1906)
George Dashiell Bayard, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Seneca Falls, New York (d. 1862)
Lyman Abbott, American theologian and author, born in Roxbury, Massachusetts (d. 1922)
Adolf Daens, Flemish priest, politician and founder of the Daensist movement, born in Aalst, Belgium (d. 1907)
Lujo Brentano, German economist and social refermer, born in Aschaffenburg, Germany (d. 1931)
Karl Schröder II, German composer, born in Quedlinburg, Prussian Saxony (d. 1935)
Gaetano Coronaro, Italian conductor, pedagogue, and composer (La Creola), born in Vicenza, Austrian Empire (d. 1908)
Graciano López Jaena, Filipino journalist (La Solidaridad), orator, social reformist, and national hero, born in Jaro, Captaincy General of the Philippines, Spanish Empire (d. 1896)
Edward Alexander MacDowell, American composer (Indian Suite), born in New York (d. 1908)
Lionel Monckton, British composer (Edwardian musical comedy), born in London, England (d. 1924)
Antoon van Welie, Dutch painter, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Andres Randolf, Danish-American actor (The Kiss), born in Viborg, Denmark (d. 1930)
Saki [Hector Hugo Munro], British writer (Reginald; When William Came), born in Akyab, British Burma (d. 1916)
Adolf Vogl, German composer, born in Munich, Germany (d. 1961)
Francis Burton Harrison, American-Filipino statesman, Governor General of the Philippines (1913-21), born in New York City (d. 1957)
Max Pallenberg [Pollack], Austrian extempore farce actor (Morn to Midnight), husband of operetta star Fritzi Massary, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1934)
Paul Klee, Swiss-German abstract painter and educator at the Bauhaus (Twittering Machine), born in Münchenbuchsee Switzerland, (d. 1940)
Gladys Cooper, British actress (My Fair Lady), born in Lewisham, England (d. 1971)
Robert Moses, American Parks official (oversaw rebuilding of Long Island & NYC parks & roads), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1981)
Edwin Howard Armstrong, American radio pioneer inventor (FM), born in New York City (d. 1954)
Fernand Collin, Belgian economist and banker, born in Antwerp (d. 1990)
Jacob Kruijt, Dutch sociologist, born in Zaandijk, Netherlands (d. 1975)

Wilf Carter, Canadian country and western singer-songwriter, yodeler, and guitarist, also known as "Montana Slim", born in Port Hilford, Nova Scotia (d. 1996)
Richard Sturzenegger, Swiss composer and cellist, born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1976)
Edwin H. "Buddy" Morris, American music publisher (Edwin H. Morris & Company), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Kam Tong, Chinese American actor (Have Gun Will Travel, Across the Pacific, Flower Drum Song), born in San Francisco (d. 1969)
Bill Holland, American auto racer (Indianapolis 500 1949; runner-up 1947, 48, 50), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1984)
Christopher Fry, English playwright (Ring Around the Moon), born in Bristol, England (d. 2005)
Celia Johnson, English actress (Happy Breed, Brief Encounter), born in Richmond, Surrey (d. 1982)
Mona Barrie [Smith], British-American actress (Dawn on Great Divide), born in London, England (d. 1964)
Abe Burrows [Borowitz], American Tony Award and Pulitzer Prize winning songwriter, composer and writer (Guys and Dolls), born in Brooklyn, New York City (d. 1985)
Eric Tindill, New Zealand rugby union halfback (1 cap; Wellington RFU), cricket wicketkeeper (5 Tests, 7 dismissals) and international umpire (1 Test), born in Nelson, New Zealand (d. 2010)
Helen Vlachos, Greek newspaper publisher (closed her newspapers in protest at military junta), born in Athens, Greece (d. 1995)
Jules Dassin, American film director (The Naked City; Night And The City; Rififi), who was blacklisted during McCarthy era and moved to France, born in Middletown, Connecticut (d. 2008)
Peg Leg Sam [Arthur Jackson], American country blues harmonica player, singer and comedian, born in Jonesville, South Carolina (d. 1977)
Benjamin O. Davis Jr., American Air Force General during WWII, born in Washington D.C. (d. 2002)
Alfred Bester, American sci-fi author (Deceivers, Starlight), born in New York City (d. 1987)
Donald Phillips, British pianist and composer (Spanish Fiesta, Concerto In Jazz), born in Dalston, London (d. 1994)
Lynn Bari [Margaret Schuyler Fisher], American actress (Shock, Nocturne, 6 Gun Law), born in Roanoke, Virginia (d. 1989)
Ray Meyer, American College-Basketball HOF coach (DePaul University 1942-84; UPI & AP Coach of the Year 1980, 84), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2006)
Saburō Takata, Japanese composer (The New Earth and Man; Marionette; Aoki-okami), born in Nagoya, Japan (d. 2000)
(Connie) "Pee Wee" Crayton, American R&B and blues guitarist and singer ("Do Unto Others"), born in Rockdale, Texas (d. 1985)
Jerry Zipkin, American socialite (friend of Nancy Reagan), born in New York City (d. 1995)
Dario Mangiarotti, Italian fencer (1 Olympic gold, 2 silver 1948, 52), born in Milan, Italy (d. 2010)
Eddie "Cleanhead" Vinson, American blues, jazz, and R&B alto saxophonist, born in Houston, Texas (d. 1988)

Raiford "Ossie" Davis, American actor, director, author, and civil rights activist (Purlie Victorious, Do the Right Thing, Hot Stuff, Man Called Adam), born in Cogdell, Clinch County, Georgia (d. 2005)
Barry Galbraith, American jazz and studio guitarist (Claude Thornhill, Coleman Hawkins, Gil Evans), and educator, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1983)
Rita Streich, Russian-German soprano, born in Barnaul, Siberia (d. 1987)
Willis Conover, American broadcaster (Voice of America), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1996)

Larry D Mann, Canadian actor and broadcaster (Accidental Family, Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2014)
Lotti van der Gaag, Dutch sculptor and painter, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1999)
Peggy Cummins [Augusta Fuller], Welsh-born Irish actress (Gun Crazy, Curse of the Demon), born in Prestatyn, Denbighshire, Wales (d. 2017)
Jock Aird, Scottish soccer defender (4 caps Scotland, 2 New Zealand; Burnley), born in Glencraig, Scotland (d. 2021)
Ramsey Clark, American lawyer, US Attorney General (1967-69) and activist, born in Dallas, Texas (d. 2021)
Roméo LeBlanc, Canadian politician, 25th Governor General of Canada (1995-99), born in Memramcook, New Brunswick (d. 2009)
Sterling Lanier, American writer (Hiero's Journey), born in New York City (d. 2007)
Józef Glemp, Polish cardinal, born in Inowrocław, Poland (d. 2013)
Gino Cimoli, American baseball player, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2011)
Bill "Moose" Skowron, American baseball first baseman (8 × MLB All-Star; World Series 1956, 58, 61, 62 NY Yankees, 1963 LA Dodgers), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Alison Plowden, English historian known for her Tudor histories, born in Quetta, India (d. 2007)
Allen Klein, American rock and roll business manager - ABKCO (Sam Cooke; Rolling Stones; Beatles), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2009)
André S. Labarthe, French actor, film producer and director (Vivre sa vie, Cinéastes de notre temps), born in Oloron-Sainte-Marie, France (d. 2018)

Gene Shue, American NBA basketball guard, 1954-64, 5X All-Star (New York Knicks, Fort Wayne/Detroit Pistons, and 2 other teams), and NBA coach, 1966-89, 2X Coach of the Year (Baltimore/Washington Bullets, Philadelphia 76ers, and 2 other teams), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2022)
Gunnel Lindblom, Swedish actress (Hunger, Virgin Spring), born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 2021)
Noel McGregor, New Zealand cricket batsman (25 Tests, 1 x 100; Otago CA), born in Dunedin, New Zealand (d. 2007)

Norm Provan, Australian rugby league second rower (14 Tests; St. George RLFC NSWRL Premiers 1956-66; 13th RL "Immortal") and coach (St. George, Parramatta, Cronulla), born in Urana, Australia (d. 2021)

Arthur Leigh Allen, American elementary school teacher and suspected Zodiac serial killer, born in Honolulu, Hawaii (d. 1992)
Boris Volynov, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 5, 21), born in Irkutsk, Siberia
Colin Baker, Welsh soccer wing half (7 caps; Cardiff City 298 games), born in Cardiff, Wales (d. 2021)
John Bingham, Lord Lucan, British peer suspected of murdering his nanny who disappeared, born in London (presumed dead)
John Douglas, Scottish rugby union No. 8 (12 Tests; Blackheath FC, Stewart's Melville RFC; Barbarians RFC), born in London, England (d. 2025)
Bonno Spieker, Dutch 2nd Chamber member (PvdA), born in Delfzijl, Netherlands (d. 2017)
Jacques Pépin, French chef, writer and TV personality (Julia and Jacques Cooking at Home), born in Bourg-en-Bresse, France
Harry Bild, Swedish soccer striker (28 caps; IFK Norrköping, Feyenoord, Östers IF), born in Växjö, Sweden (d. 2025)
Malcolm Kirk, English professional wrestler known as "King Kong Kirk", born in Streethouse, Yorkshire (d. 1987)
Roger E. Mosley, American actor (actor (Magnum, P.I. -"TC"; Leadbelly), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2022)
[Bryan] Chas Chandler, English rock bassist (The Animals - "House of the Rising Sun"), and manager of Jimi Hendrix, born in Newcastle upon Tyne, England (d. 1996)
Harold E. Varmus, American scientist (1989 Nobel Prize for Medicine or Physiology for cellular origin of retroviral oncogenes.), born in Oceanside, New York
Michael J. Moorcock, English sci-fi author (Alien Heat, Bull & Spear), born in London, England
Sandro Lopopolo Italian lightweight boxer (Olympic-Silver-1960), born in Milan, Italy (d. 2014)
Wiesje Backer, Dutch ballet dancer, actress. and playwright, born in Ammerzoden, Netherlands (d. 2018)
Bramwell Morrison, Canadian member of children's music group Sharon, Lois & Bram, born in Toronto, Ontario
Ilario Castagner, Italian soccer manager (Perugia, Lazio, AC Milan, Internazionale, Ascoli) and striker (Perugia, AC Prato), born in Vittorio Veneto, Italy (d. 2023)
Jan Kowalczyk, Polish equestrian rider (Olympics gold show jumping individual; silver team 1980), born in Drogomyśl, Poland (d. 2020)
Sam Andrew, American guitarist, singer-songwriter (Big Brother and the Holding Company; Kozmic Blues Band), and classical composer, born in Taft, California (d. 2015)
Wadada Leo Smith, American trumpeter and composer ("Divine Love"), born in Leland, Mississippi
William, English Prince of Gloucester and grandson of George V, born in Hadley Common, England (d. 1972)
Harvey Atkin, Canadian actor (Meatballs), born in Toronto, Canada (d. 2017)
Alan Rudolph, American actor and director (Endangers Species, Love at Large, Made in Heaven), born in Los Angeles, California
Bobby Keys, American session and touring rock saxophonist (Rolling Stones; Harry Nilsson; John Lennon), born in Slaton, Texas (d. 2014)
(Roger) "Deke" Leonard, Welsh rock musician (Man), born in Llanelli, Wales (d. 2017)
Carolyn Wood, American swimmer (Olympic gold 4 x 100m freestyle relay 1960), born in Portland, Oregon
